---
title: "Where NAIF fits in the Swiss Science Council's roadmap for research libraries"
subtitle: "The SSC report proposes three recommendations to transform research libraries into data hubs. NAIF already addresses key parts of that agenda."
date: 2026-04-08
lang: en-UK
author:
  - name: "Dr Moritz Mähr"
    orcid: "0000-0002-1367-1618"
    affiliation: "ETH Zurich"
description: "The Swiss Science Council's three recommendations for research libraries -- a national strategy, dedicated funding, and stronger governance -- map closely onto what NAIF is already building. This post traces the connections and argues for structural alignment."
categories:
  - "General"
tags:
  - "Research libraries"
  - "Governance"
  - "Interoperability"
proofread: false
image: "images/swr-report-page.png"
image-alt: "Screenshot of the federal press release page announcing the Swiss Science Council report Research Libraries as Data Hubs"
image-caption: "Screenshot of the federal press release page for the Swiss Science Council report 'Research Libraries as Data Hubs'. Source: [geo.admin.ch](https://www.geo.admin.ch/en/newnsb/IKzegebj8OYzThf7MEq3E). Rights: Swiss federal authorities / Swiss Science Council."
toc: true
draft: true
---

## The report in brief

In December 2025, the Swiss Science Council (SSC) published [*Research Libraries as Data Hubs*](https://cms.news.admin.ch/dam/en/swr/7gwxr5zJzfbC/2025_12_swr_research_libraries_as_data_hubs.pdf). The report makes the case that research libraries must evolve from access points for literature into strategic infrastructures that curate, connect, and preserve reliable knowledge. It identifies challenges ranging from metadata fragmentation and AI to the "shadow value" problem -- the fact that library infrastructure has become so seamlessly digital that its underlying work is largely invisible to users and decision-makers.

The report concludes with three formal recommendations. This post maps each of them onto work already underway in the NAIF project, and argues that the two initiatives would benefit from closer structural alignment.

## Recommendation I: a national strategy via SLiNER

The SSC recommends that swissuniversities provide the Swiss Library Network for Education and Research (SLiNER) with the mandate to develop a national strategy for the further digital transformation of research libraries. That strategy should address artificial intelligence, access to reliable information, historical depth, collaboration and governance, visibility, and training.

### Where NAIF fits

NAIF does not cover all six challenge areas, but it directly addresses two of the most technically demanding ones: **collaboration and governance** (Track 2) and **ensuring access to reliable information** through metadata interoperability (Tracks 3 and 4).

**Track 2** reviews international governance models -- federated versus centralised architectures, funding mechanisms, secondary publication rights -- and develops national scenarios for Switzerland. Through workshops with international experts and stakeholder consultations, it gathers the requirements a credible governance model must satisfy.

**Track 3** analyses Swiss institutional repositories, benchmarks their metadata against international standards (OpenAIRE, Dublin Core, DataCite), and produces a gap-analysis report with harmonisation recommendations.

**Track 4** focuses on the enrichment layer: persistent identifiers such as ORCID and ROR, funding metadata, and Open Access status fields. The deliverables -- mappings, authoritative lists, implementation guidance -- are precisely the kind of practical standards work a national strategy would need to draw on.

### Towards one structure, not two

The report notes that SLiNER currently lacks the mandate, resources, and implementation capacity to drive innovation at the national scale. It operates as a militia organisation with only guest status in bodies such as the Open Science Delegation.

NAIF's planned **national working group**, meanwhile, is designed as the project's unified outcome across all four tracks. It will define organisational form, representativeness criteria, and a shared strategy for dissemination and findability of Swiss research outputs -- with the explicit aim of sustaining interoperability progress beyond the project period.

Both structures address the same gap: Switzerland lacks a permanent, resourced coordination mechanism for research library interoperability. Rather than building two parallel bodies, there is a clear opportunity to **consolidate the NAIF working group with SLiNER into one consistent structure** that carries both the technical interoperability mandate NAIF has developed and the broader strategic mandate the SSC envisions. NAIF's findings on governance scenarios, metadata harmonisation, and stakeholder requirements could feed directly into SLiNER's strengthened mandate, while SLiNER's institutional anchoring within swissuniversities could provide the sustained legitimacy and coordination capacity that a project-bound working group cannot guarantee on its own. The alternative -- separate structures with overlapping scope and limited resources -- would reproduce the fragmentation both initiatives set out to overcome.

## Recommendation II: dedicated funding in the ERI Dispatch 2029--2032

The SSC recommends that dedicated funding for research libraries be included in the next Education, Research, and Innovation Dispatch (2029--2032), whether through a new innovation fund, a specific PgB programme for research libraries, or funding under Article 15 of the RIPA.

The context is urgent. Since 2000, Projektgebundene Beiträge (PgB) have been the primary mechanism for national library collaboration in Switzerland. They enabled the Consortium of Swiss Academic Libraries and the Swiss Library Service Platform (SLSP). But in September 2024, the Federal Council announced cost-cutting measures that could discontinue PgB funding altogether.

### NAIF as a case study

NAIF is funded by swissuniversities and eight participating higher education institutions -- squarely within the PgB ecosystem. It demonstrates what this funding model can achieve: a nationally coordinated, multi-institutional project that addresses a practical infrastructure gap no single institution could solve alone.

The project also illustrates the PgB model's limitations. PgB requires funded entities to cover at least 50 per cent of project costs, and projects must eventually become self-sustaining. The SSC report observes that this seed-funding logic can discourage disruptive innovation, because institutions gravitate towards incremental extensions of existing services. NAIF's working-group model is a deliberate attempt to create a sustainable structure that outlasts the project period -- but whether it can do so without continued funding remains an open question.

If the PgB mechanism is abolished and no equivalent takes its place, the type of cross-institutional coordination NAIF represents would lose its financial basis. The project thus serves as both a proof of concept for the SSC's funding recommendation and a concrete illustration of what is at stake if it is not followed.

## Recommendation III: stronger governance and institutional integration

The SSC recommends that Swiss higher education institutions involve their libraries more closely in strategic processes and integrate them into the relevant committees. It also calls for greater involvement of research libraries in national strategic bodies to ensure the library perspective is adequately considered in research and infrastructure policy.

### What NAIF contributes

**Track 1** connects the repository interoperability agenda to research assessment policy. By linking metadata quality work with DORA, CoARA, and the Swiss Year of Scientometrics community, it shows that repository infrastructure is not a purely technical concern but has direct implications for how research is evaluated and reported.

This matters because one of the report's recurring themes is the **shadow value** problem. Research libraries have integrated their services so seamlessly into the digital landscape that their foundational work -- standardising, documenting, curating metadata -- has become invisible to researchers and institutional leaders. Users bypass the physical library, access resources directly online, and fail to see the infrastructure required behind the scenes. As the SSC puts it, infrastructures typically only become visible on instances of breakdown.

NAIF's metadata and interoperability work is a textbook example of this invisible but essential labour. When ORCID identifiers are correctly linked to institutional outputs, when repository metadata follow shared standards, when Open Access status is machine-readable across systems, the result is findable research. But none of these improvements are visible to the end user in the way a new library building or a reading room renovation would be.

The SSC argues that libraries must invest considerable effort in communicating their social productivity and capitalised value. NAIF can contribute to this by producing **measurable deliverables** -- the gap-analysis report (Track 3), the harmonisation mappings (Track 4), the governance scenarios (Track 2) -- that make the current state of fragmentation and the cost of inaction concrete and legible to decision-makers. Making the invisible visible is not a communications exercise alone; it requires the kind of structured evidence NAIF is designed to produce.

## What NAIF does not cover

The report addresses several themes that fall outside NAIF's scope. These include digital preservation and "bit rot", copyright and licensing frameworks for AI training, professional development and upskilling of library staff, and the broader role of libraries in teaching information literacy. NAIF does not claim to be a comprehensive answer to the data hubs agenda. It works on a narrower but essential subset: the metadata, interoperability, and governance conditions without which the broader agenda remains aspirational.

## Looking ahead

The Swiss Science Council's report and the NAIF project approach the same problem from different levels. The report defines the strategic case and policy framework; NAIF works on practical implementation in the repository domain. The three SSC recommendations -- a national strategy, dedicated funding, and stronger governance -- each have a clear counterpart in NAIF's track structure and working-group model.

The most consequential next step would be to make these connections explicit at the institutional level: consolidating NAIF's working group with a strengthened SLiNER into a single, coherent governance structure; treating NAIF as evidence in the ERI Dispatch funding debate; and using the project's deliverables to communicate the value of infrastructure work that too often remains invisible.

## Sources

- Swiss Science Council. (2025). *Research Libraries as Data Hubs*. <https://cms.news.admin.ch/dam/en/swr/7gwxr5zJzfbC/2025_12_swr_research_libraries_as_data_hubs.pdf>
- Swiss federal authorities. (2025). *Research Libraries as Data Hubs -- New Report from the Swiss Science Council SSC*. <https://www.geo.admin.ch/en/newnsb/IKzegebj8OYzThf7MEq3E>
- NAIF project website: *About*. <https://eth-library.github.io/naif/about.html>
